title: Mean and standard deviation of a sample
prompt:
Every data-analysis pipeline starts here. A measurement column is given in **A2:A21** (20 surface-roughness readings, in µm). Compute the **count**, the **sample mean** $\bar x$ and the **sample standard deviation** $s$.

These three numbers feed every later step (Chauvenet, $\chi^2$, $t$-test, …), so get them right.
hint: Use STDEV.S (the n−1 sample form), not STDEV.P.

checks.label: Count n
checks.teach: COUNT returns how many numeric cells are in the range. Here $n=20$. Every $1/(2n)$ and degrees-of-freedom term downstream uses this.

checks.label: Mean x̄
checks.teach: The sample mean $\bar x=\frac1n\sum x_i = 44.90\ \mu m$. This is the best estimate of the true value once outliers are removed.

checks.label: St. dev. s
checks.teach: **STDEV.S** divides by $n-1$ (Bessel's correction) and estimates the population $\sigma$ from a *sample*: $s=3.01\ \mu m$. STDEV.P (divide by $n$) is only for a full population and is the classic exam trap.

method: $n=$ COUNT $=20$; $\bar x=$ AVERAGE $=44.90\,\mu m$; $s=$ STDEV.S $=3.01\,\mu m$. Always use the *sample* standard deviation STDEV.S for experimental data.

title: Outlier management — Chauvenet's criterion (exam capstone)
prompt:
**This is the 8-point Data-Analysis exam question.** The 20 roughness readings of the previous exercise are in **A2:A21**. Apply **Chauvenet's exclusion principle**.

Chauvenet says a single reading is suspect if the expected number of samples at least as extreme is below $\tfrac12$, i.e. its tail probability is below $\alpha=\dfrac{1}{2n}$. Working two-sided, each tail gets $\dfrac{1}{4n}$, so the **standardized exclusion factor** is
$$z=\Phi^{-1}\!\left(1-\frac{1}{4n}\right)=\text{NORM.S.INV}(1-1/(4n)).$$
The exclusion limits are $\bar x\mp z\,s$. Any reading outside $[x_L^{min},\,x_L^{max}]$ is an outlier.
hint: α = 1/(2n) = 0.025. The factor uses NORM.S.INV(1−1/(4n)) — a one-sided 1−1/(4n) quantile.

checks.label: n
checks.teach: $n=20$ over the whole experiment (all four operators × five parts).

checks.label: s
checks.teach: $s=3.01\ \mu m$ with STDEV.S. **Note:** the official Chauvenet limits use the *raw-sample* mean and st. dev. — before removing any point.

checks.label: Risk α
checks.teach: The per-datum risk of being wrongly excluded is $\alpha=\frac{1}{2n}=\frac1{40}=0.025$. Report it as a probability (0.025), **not** as 2.5 %.

checks.label: Exclusion factor z
checks.teach: Two-sided, each tail carries $\frac{1}{4n}=0.0125$, so the standardized cut is the $1-0.0125=0.9875$ quantile of the standard normal: $z=2.241$.

checks.label: Upper limit xL max
checks.teach: $x_L^{max}=\bar x + z\,s = 51.64\ \mu m$. Equivalently $=\text{NORM.INV}(1-1/(4n),\bar x,s)$.

checks.label: min
checks.teach: The smallest reading is **36** µm (Operator 4, part 1).

checks.label: max
checks.teach: The largest reading is 48 µm, which sits comfortably inside the upper limit 51.64.

checks.label: Outlier?
checks.teach: $36 < 38.16$, so **Yes**, there is an outlier — and its value is **36 µm**. It should be removed and the statistics recomputed on the remaining 19 data.

title: Chauvenet on a large dataset — the NORM.INV shortcut
prompt:
In the full lab the dataset has **n = 50** values with mean $\bar x=4.50618$ mm and $s=0.0025677$ mm (already computed and given). Reproduce the Chauvenet exclusion limits the way the reference workbook does it: directly with **NORM.INV**, which already embeds the mean and st. dev.

Limit $=\text{NORM.INV}\!\left(p,\bar x,s\right)$ with $p=\dfrac{1}{4n}$ for the lower and $p=1-\dfrac{1}{4n}$ for the upper.
hint: PxL = 1/(4n) = 1/200 = 0.005. NORM.INV(p, mean, s) gives the limit straight away.

checks.label: PxL
checks.teach: With $n=50$, the single-tail probability is $\frac{1}{4n}=0.005$. (The whole-experiment risk $\frac{1}{2n}=0.01$ is split over the two tails.)

checks.label: Lower limit
checks.teach: NORM.INV($0.005,\bar x,s$) returns the value with 0.5 % of the normal mass below it: $4.49957$ mm. This equals $\bar x - 2.576\,s$.

checks.label: Upper limit
checks.teach: NORM.INV($0.995,\bar x,s$) = $4.51280$ mm $=\bar x+2.576\,s$. Using NORM.INV(p, mean, s) is equivalent to $\bar x\mp\text{NORM.S.INV}(1-1/(4n))\,s$ — pick whichever the exam wording suggests.

method: $P_{xL}=1/(4n)=0.005$. $x_L^{min}=\text{NORM.INV}(0.005,4.50618,0.0025677)=4.49957$; $x_L^{max}=\text{NORM.INV}(0.995,\dots)=4.51280$. None of the 50 readings lies outside, so no exclusion is needed here.

title: Outlier management — IQR / box-plot rule
prompt:
The **box-plot (Tukey) rule** is a distribution-free alternative to Chauvenet. Using the same 20 roughness readings in **A2:A21**, compute the quartiles and the inner fences.

$Q_1=\text{QUARTILE}(range,1)$, $Q_3=\text{QUARTILE}(range,3)$, $IQR=Q_3-Q_1$. The fences are $Q_1-1.5\,IQR$ (lower) and $Q_3+1.5\,IQR$ (upper); anything outside is flagged.
hint: QUARTILE's second argument is the quartile number: 1 for Q1, 3 for Q3.

checks.label: Q1
checks.teach: $Q_1$ is the 25th percentile: a quarter of the data lie below it. Here $Q_1=43.75\ \mu m$.

checks.label: Q3
checks.teach: $Q_3$ is the 75th percentile, $=47\ \mu m$. The middle 50 % of the data live in $[Q_1,Q_3]$.

checks.label: IQR
checks.teach: $IQR=Q_3-Q_1=3.25\ \mu m$ — a robust spread measure that ignores the tails (so a single wild point cannot inflate it the way $s$ would).

checks.label: Lower fence
checks.teach: Lower inner fence $=Q_1-1.5\,IQR=43.75-4.875=38.875\ \mu m$.

checks.label: Upper fence
checks.teach: Upper inner fence $=Q_3+1.5\,IQR=51.875\ \mu m$.

checks.label: Outlier?
checks.teach: $36<38.875$, so the box-plot rule flags **36** too — both methods agree here. The $1.5\,IQR$ multiplier corresponds to roughly $\pm2.7\sigma$ for a normal distribution.

method: $Q_1=43.75$, $Q_3=47$, $IQR=3.25$. Fences $43.75-4.875=38.875$ and $47+4.875=51.875$. The value 36 lies below 38.875 → flagged. Box-plot and Chauvenet both identify 36.

title: Frequency distribution — building the classes
prompt:
To test the shape of the distribution we group the data into classes. The reference workbook spans the **examined field** $\bar x\pm4s$ and uses **$\sqrt{n}+4$** classes.

For the full lab set ($n=50$, $\bar x=4.50618$ mm, $s=0.0025677$ mm), compute the field limits, the number of classes and the class width.
hint: Number of classes = SQRT(n)+4. Class width = (max field − min field) / number of classes.

checks.label: Field min
checks.teach: The field is taken $\pm4s$ around the mean so that essentially all of a normal sample falls inside: $x_{min}=4.49591$ mm.

checks.label: Number of classes
checks.teach: The professor's empirical rule $\sqrt{n}+4$ gives $\approx11.07$, i.e. **11 classes** in practice. Too few classes hides structure; too many leaves empty bins that break $\chi^2$.

checks.label: Class width
checks.teach: Width $=\dfrac{x_{max}-x_{min}}{\#classes}=0.0018554$ mm. Each class then runs from a lower bound to lower + width.

title: χ² goodness-of-fit to a normal
prompt:
Test whether a sample of **n = 100** parts (mean 50, st. dev. 5) follows a **normal** distribution. The six class bounds are in **A2:B7** (from / to) and the **observed** counts $O_i$ in **C2:C7**.

For each class compute the **expected** count $E_i = n\,[\,\Phi(to)-\Phi(from)\,]$ with $\Phi=\text{NORM.DIST}(\cdot,\bar x,s,1)$, then $\dfrac{(O_i-E_i)^2}{E_i}$, sum to get $\chi^2$, and compare with the critical value.
hint: Cumulative NORM.DIST (last arg = 1) gives Φ. Expected = n·(Φ(to) − Φ(from)). dof = classes − 1 − (params estimated = 2).

checks.label: Expected E (class 1)
checks.teach: Expected = total $n$ times the normal probability of falling in the class: $100\,[\Phi(45)-\Phi(-\infty)]=15.87$. The cumulative flag (1) is essential — point density (0) would be wrong here.

checks.label: Expected E (class 2)
checks.teach: $100[\Phi(47.5)-\Phi(45)]=14.99$ expected parts in class 2.

checks.label: Expected E (class 3)
checks.teach: The central class around the mean carries the most mass: 19.15 expected.

checks.label: Expected E (class 4)
checks.teach: By symmetry class 4 mirrors class 3: 19.15.

checks.label: Expected E (class 5)
checks.teach: 14.99, mirroring class 2.

checks.label: Expected E (class 6)
checks.teach: 15.87 in the far-right open class, mirroring class 1.

checks.label: Experimental χ²
checks.teach: $\chi^2=\sum\frac{(O_i-E_i)^2}{E_i}$ measures total mismatch. Small $\chi^2=0.96$ means observed and expected counts are very close.

checks.label: Degrees of freedom
checks.teach: dof $=$ (number of classes) $-1-$ (parameters estimated from the data). We estimated **two** parameters ($\bar x$ and $s$), so dof $=6-1-2=3$. Forgetting to subtract the estimated parameters is a classic mistake.

checks.label: Critical χ²
checks.teach: At a 5 % significance the right-tail critical value with 3 dof is 7.815. CHISQ.INV.RT(α, dof) returns it directly.

checks.label: Normality decision
checks.teach: $\chi^2_{exp}=0.96 < 7.815$, so we **do not reject** normality — the data are consistent with a Gaussian. We reject only if $\chi^2$ exceeds the critical value.

checks.label: p-value
checks.teach: The p-value $=P(\chi^2_3>0.96)=0.81$: an 81 % chance of seeing this much (or more) deviation by chance alone, far above 5 % — strong support for normality.

method: Expected counts $E_i=n[\Phi(to)-\Phi(from)]$. $\chi^2=\sum(O_i-E_i)^2/E_i=0.96$. dof $=6-1-2=3$, critical $=7.815$. $0.96<7.815$ → accept normality; p-value $=0.81$.

title: χ² acceptance band — two-sided test for systematic effects
prompt:
The reference workbook tests the frequency distribution **two-sided** at an 80 % confidence level with **8 degrees of freedom**, and accepts normality (no systematic effect) when the experimental $\chi^2$ lies *between* a lower and an upper boundary.

Given the confidence level and dof, compute both $\chi^2$ boundaries and decide for the workbook's experimental value $\chi^2_{exp}=9.12$.
hint: Two-sided: lower = CHISQ.INV((1−CL)/2, dof), upper = CHISQ.INV(1−(1−CL)/2, dof).

checks.label: Lower boundary
checks.teach: With CL = 80 %, the total risk 20 % is split into 10 % per tail. The lower boundary is the 10 % quantile: CHISQ.INV(0.1, 8) = 3.49.

checks.label: Upper boundary
checks.teach: The upper boundary is the 90 % quantile: CHISQ.INV(0.9, 8) = 13.36. A χ² *too small* also matters here — it would mean the fit is suspiciously perfect (possible data tampering or wrong dof).

checks.label: Decision
checks.teach: $3.49 < 9.12 < 13.36$, so the experimental $\chi^2$ is **inside** the band → the null hypothesis of a normal distribution is *not* rejected and no systematic effect is evidenced (accepting a 20 % risk of a first-kind error).

method: Two-sided 80 % band with 8 dof: $[\chi^2_{0.10},\chi^2_{0.90}]=[3.49,\,13.36]$. Experimental 9.12 is inside → normality accepted, no systematic effect.

title: Normal probability plot — plotting positions & quantiles
prompt:
A **normal probability plot (NPP)** checks normality graphically: sort the data, assign each a plotting position $p_i=\dfrac{i-0.5}{n}$, and convert it to a theoretical standard-normal quantile $z_i=\text{NORM.S.INV}(p_i)$. If the points $(z_i,\;x_{(i)})$ fall on a straight line, the data are normal.

The **ordered** sample (10 values) is in **A2:A11**. Fill the rank $i$, $p_i$ and $z_i$ for the indicated rows, and the mean/st. dev.
hint: Plotting position p = (i − 0.5)/n. The theoretical quantile is NORM.S.INV(p).

checks.label: n
checks.teach: $n=10$ ordered values.

checks.label: mean
checks.teach: The line fitted in an NPP has slope $\approx s$ and intercept $\approx\bar x$; here $\bar x=45.99$ µm.

checks.label: st. dev.
checks.teach: $s=3.24$ µm — the expected slope of the NPP line.

checks.label: p for i=1
checks.teach: The first (smallest) value gets $p=\frac{1-0.5}{10}=0.05$. The $-0.5$ continuity correction keeps the extremes off $0$ and $1$ (where $z$ would be $\pm\infty$).

checks.label: z for i=1
checks.teach: $z_1=\text{NORM.S.INV}(0.05)=-1.645$: the smallest reading is expected about 1.6 st. dev. below the mean.

checks.label: p for i=5
checks.teach: The 5th value: $p=\frac{4.5}{10}=0.45$, just below the median.

checks.label: z for i=5
checks.teach: $z=\text{NORM.S.INV}(0.45)=-0.126$, near the centre of the plot.

checks.label: p for i=10
checks.teach: The largest value: $p=\frac{9.5}{10}=0.95$.

checks.label: z for i=10
checks.teach: $z_{10}=+1.645$. Because $p_i$ and $1-p_i$ are symmetric, the $z$ values are antisymmetric. Curvature of the $(z,x)$ points (an S-shape) would signal skew or heavy tails.

method: Plotting position $p_i=(i-0.5)/n$, quantile $z_i=\text{NORM.S.INV}(p_i)$. Ends: $p_1=0.05\to z=-1.645$; $p_{10}=0.95\to z=+1.645$. A straight $(z_i,x_{(i)})$ scatter ⇒ normal, with slope $\approx s$ and intercept $\approx\bar x$.

title: Hypothesis test on a mean (one-sample t-test)
prompt:
After removing outliers we test whether the process mean equals a **nominal value** $\mu_0$. The 20 roughness readings are in **A2:A21**; the nominal target is $\mu_0=46$ µm (in **F1**).

Compute the $t$-statistic $t=\dfrac{\bar x-\mu_0}{s/\sqrt n}$, the two-tailed critical value at 95 % confidence, the decision, and the p-value.
hint: t = (x̄ − μ0)/(s/√n). Critical value = T.INV.2T(0.05, n−1). Reject if |t| > critical.

checks.label: s
checks.teach: $s=3.01$ µm. The **standard error of the mean** is $s/\sqrt n=3.01/\sqrt{20}=0.673$ µm.

checks.label: t statistic
checks.teach: $t=\dfrac{\bar x-\mu_0}{s/\sqrt n}=\dfrac{44.90-46}{0.673}=-1.636$. The sign says the sample mean is below the target; only the magnitude matters for a two-sided test.

checks.label: Critical t
checks.teach: With $n-1=19$ dof, the two-tailed 95 % critical value is $\pm2.093$. **T.INV.2T(0.05, 19)** already returns the positive bound.

checks.label: Decision
checks.teach: $|{-1.636}|=1.636 < 2.093$, so we **do not reject** $H_0$: the data are consistent with $\mu=46$ µm at the 95 % level. The difference $-1.1$ µm is within sampling noise.

checks.label: p-value
checks.teach: The two-tailed p-value $=P(|T_{19}|>1.636)=0.118$. Since $0.118>0.05$ we keep $H_0$ — consistent with the critical-value decision.

title: One-way ANOVA — F-test for an operator effect
prompt:
**ANOVA** asks whether the differences *between* operators are bigger than the random scatter *within* each operator. The five operator columns (10 measurements each) are in **D11:H20**.

Compute the column means and within-column variances, then the between- and within-mean-squares, the variance ratio $F$, the critical $F$ at 80 % confidence and the decision.
hint: MS_between = VAR.S(column means)·(rows per column). MS_within = AVERAGE of the column variances. F = MS_between/MS_within. Critical = F.INV.RT(0.2, df1, df2).

checks.label: Mean Oper.1
checks.teach: Average of the 10 readings of Operator 1.

checks.label: Mean Oper.2
checks.teach: Operator 2 mean — the column means are the heart of the between-groups variation.

checks.label: Mean Oper.3
checks.teach: Operator 3 mean.

checks.label: Mean Oper.4
checks.teach: Operator 4 mean — visibly the highest, the chief suspect for a systematic effect.

checks.label: Mean Oper.5
checks.teach: Operator 5 mean.

checks.label: dof between
checks.teach: Between-groups dof $=k-1=5-1=4$ (number of columns minus one).

checks.label: dof within
checks.teach: Within (error) dof $=N-k=49-4=45$, the leftover after the between term.

checks.label: MS between
checks.teach: Between-groups mean square $=$ (variance of the 5 column means) $\times$ (rows per column). Multiplying by 10 rescales the spread of *means* up to the spread of *single* observations.

checks.label: MS within
checks.teach: Within-groups mean square $=$ the pooled (average) of the five column variances — the pure random-error variance.

checks.label: F ratio
checks.teach: $F=\dfrac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}}=5.38$. If operators had no effect, $F$ would hover around 1; a large $F$ means between-operator differences exceed the noise.

checks.label: Critical F
checks.teach: At 80 % confidence the right-tail critical $F$ with (4, 45) dof is 1.565. (Equivalently F.INV(0.8, 4, 45).) The risk of a first-kind error is the deliberately high 20 %.

checks.label: Decision
checks.teach: $F=5.38 > 1.565$, so the variance ratio exceeds the critical value: with a 20 % risk the **operator differences are systematic**, not random. The process needs operator-procedure review.

method: Column means → between-MS $=\text{VAR.S(means)}\times10=2.611\times10^{-5}$; within-MS $=$ avg column variance $=4.858\times10^{-6}$. $F=5.38$, critical $F_{0.2}(4,45)=1.565$. $F>F_{crit}$ ⇒ systematic operator effect (risk 20 %).

title: ANOVA decision via the p-value
prompt: Same ANOVA outcome, read the other way. Given the variance ratio $F=5.376$ with $df_1=4$ (between) and $df_2=45$ (within), compute the **right-tail p-value** $P(F_{4,45}>5.376)$ and decide at a 20 % risk.
hint: p = F.DIST.RT(F, df1, df2). Reject H0 if p < α (here α = 0.20).

checks.label: p-value
checks.teach: $p=P(F_{4,45}>5.376)=0.00126$: only a 0.13 % chance of an $F$ this large if operators truly had no effect. F.DIST.RT gives the right-tail (upper) probability directly.

checks.label: Decision
checks.teach: $p=0.00126 < 0.20$, so we **reject** $H_0$ (no operator effect): the effect is systematic. The p-value and critical-value methods always agree — $p<\alpha$ exactly when $F>F_{crit}$.

method: $p=\text{F.DIST.RT}(5.376,4,45)=0.00126$. Since $p<\alpha=0.20$, reject the null of equal operator means: the operator effect is systematic.

title: Linear regression — least-squares slope, intercept, prediction
prompt:
A calibration experiment relates the controlled variable $t$ (temperature, °C, in **A2:A7**) to the response $x$ (output, mV, in **B2:B7**). Fit the least-squares line $x=a+b\,t$.

There is no SLOPE function here, so build it from sums: with $\bar t,\bar x$ the means,
$$b=\frac{\sum (t_i-\bar t)(x_i-\bar x)}{\sum (t_i-\bar t)^2},\qquad a=\bar x-b\,\bar t.$$
Helper columns are provided in **E** (cross-products) and **F** (squared deviations). Then predict $x$ at $t=25$ and the residual of the $t=20$ point.
hint: Slope b = SUM(cross-products)/SUM(squared deviations). Intercept a = x̄ − b·t̄. Predicted x = a + b·t.

checks.label: n
checks.teach: Six calibration points.

checks.label: t̄
checks.teach: $\bar t=25$ °C — the centroid the regression line must pass through.

checks.label: x̄
checks.teach: $\bar x=1.0033$ mV. The line always passes through $(\bar t,\bar x)$.

checks.label: cross-product row 1
checks.teach: $(t_1-\bar t)(x_1-\bar x)=(0-25)(0-1.0033)=25.08$. Summing these over all rows gives the numerator of the slope.

checks.label: squared deviation row 1
checks.teach: $(t_1-\bar t)^2=625$. Their sum is the slope denominator and measures how spread out the inputs are.

checks.label: Σ cross
checks.teach: $\sum(t_i-\bar t)(x_i-\bar x)=70.2$ — the covariation of $t$ and $x$.

checks.label: slope b
checks.teach: $b=\dfrac{70.2}{1750}=0.04011$ mV/°C — the sensitivity of the instrument.

checks.label: intercept a
checks.teach: $a=\bar x-b\bar t=1.0033-0.04011\cdot25=0.00048$ mV — essentially zero offset, as expected for a good sensor.

checks.label: predicted x(25)
checks.teach: $\hat x(25)=a+b\cdot25=1.0033$ mV. At $t=\bar t$ the prediction equals $\bar x$ — a useful sanity check.

checks.label: residual at t=20
checks.teach: Residual $=x_{obs}-\hat x=0.80-(a+20b)=0.80-0.8023=-0.0023$ mV. Residuals should scatter randomly around 0 with no trend; a pattern means the linear model is inadequate.

title: Es01 worked example — descriptive statistics of the raw data
prompt:
This is the **real lab dataset (Es01)**. Five operators each measured the same part **ten** times; the **50 raw readings** (in mm) are stacked in **A2:A51** (Operator 1 first, then 2, 3, 4, 5).

Before any cleaning, characterize the raw sample: the **count** $n$, the **mean** $\bar x$, the **sample standard deviation** $s$ (STDEV.S), the **minimum**, **maximum** and the two **quartiles** $Q_1$, $Q_3$. These exact numbers are what the workbook's *Data* sheet reports and they feed the Chauvenet step next.
hint: Use STDEV.S (the n−1 sample form). QUARTILE's second argument is 1 for Q1, 3 for Q3.

checks.label: Count n
checks.teach: The whole experiment has $n=50$ data (5 operators $\times$ 10 measurements). Every $1/(4n)$ and degrees-of-freedom term downstream uses this.

checks.label: Mean x̄
checks.teach: $\bar x=\frac1n\sum x_i = 4.506192\ \text{mm}$ — the *raw* mean, computed before any outlier is removed. This is the centre used to build the Chauvenet limits.

checks.label: St. dev. s
checks.teach: **STDEV.S** divides by $n-1$ and estimates $\sigma$ from a sample: $s=0.0029867$ mm. The single most common slip is STDEV.P (divide by $n$), which under-states the spread.

checks.label: Minimum
checks.teach: The smallest reading is **4.4988** mm (Operator 1, measurement 4) — a candidate low outlier.

checks.label: Maximum
checks.teach: The largest reading is **4.5139** mm (Operator 5, measurement 7) — a candidate high outlier. Chauvenet will tell us which, if either, to discard.

checks.label: Q1
checks.teach: $Q_1=4.504025$ mm: a quarter of the data lie below it.

checks.label: Q3
checks.teach: $Q_3=4.5079$ mm: three quarters lie below it. The middle 50 % of the data live in $[Q_1,Q_3]$.

method: On the raw 50 readings: $n=$ COUNT $=50$; $\bar x=$ AVERAGE $=4.506192$ mm; $s=$ STDEV.S $=0.0029867$ mm; min $=4.4988$, max $=4.5139$; $Q_1=4.504025$, $Q_3=4.5079$ mm. These reproduce the workbook's *Data* sheet exactly.

title: Es01 worked example — Chauvenet outlier check on the raw data
prompt:
Run **Chauvenet's exclusion principle** on the same 50 raw readings (**A2:A51**), exactly as the workbook's *Escl.* sheet does it.

The per-tail probability is $P_{xL}=\dfrac{1}{4n}$. The workbook builds the exclusion limits directly with **NORM.INV**, which already embeds the raw mean and st. dev.:
$$x_L^{min}=\text{NORM.INV}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{4n},\bar x,s\right),\qquad x_L^{max}=\text{NORM.INV}\!\left(1-\tfrac{1}{4n},\bar x,s\right).$$
Any reading outside $[x_L^{min},x_L^{max}]$ is a measurement accident to be excluded.
hint: PxL = 1/(4n) = 1/200 = 0.005. NORM.INV(p, mean, s) gives the limit straight away. Compare MIN and MAX against the limits.

checks.label: n
checks.teach: $n=50$ over the whole experiment (5 operators $\times$ 10 measurements).

checks.label: Raw mean
checks.teach: The Chauvenet limits use the **raw** mean and st. dev., computed before removing anything: $\bar x=4.506192$ mm.

checks.label: Raw st. dev.
checks.teach: $s=0.0029867$ mm (STDEV.S). Using STDEV.P here would shift both limits and is a marks-loser.

checks.label: PxL
checks.teach: Chauvenet's whole-experiment risk $\frac{1}{2n}$ is split over the two tails, so each tail carries $P_{xL}=\frac{1}{4n}=\frac{1}{200}=0.005$. Report it as a probability, not a percentage.

checks.label: Lower limit
checks.teach: NORM.INV($0.005,\bar x,s$) returns the value with 0.5 % of the normal mass below it: $x_L^{min}=4.498499$ mm. Equivalently $\bar x-z\,s$ with $z=\text{NORM.S.INV}(0.995)=2.576$.

checks.label: Upper limit
checks.teach: NORM.INV($0.995,\bar x,s$) $=x_L^{max}=4.513885$ mm $=\bar x+2.576\,s$. Readings must lie inside $[4.498499,\,4.513885]$.

checks.label: min
checks.teach: The smallest reading 4.4988 mm is **above** the lower limit 4.498499 — only just — so it is **kept**.

checks.label: max
checks.teach: The largest reading 4.5139 mm **exceeds** the upper limit 4.513885, so it is the suspect.

checks.label: Outlier?
checks.teach: $4.5139 > 4.513885$, so **Yes** — there is exactly one outlier (the maximum). Note the minimum 4.4988 is *not* excluded: Chauvenet is borderline and only the high tail crosses the limit, matching the workbook's *Escl.* sheet.

checks.label: Outlier value
checks.teach: The excluded value is the **maximum, 4.5139 mm** (Operator 5, measurement 7). It is removed and the statistics recomputed next.

method: $n=50$, raw $\bar x=4.506192$, $s=0.0029867$ mm. $P_{xL}=1/(4n)=0.005$; limits $\text{NORM.INV}(0.005/0.995,\bar x,s)=[4.498499,\,4.513885]$ mm. The maximum $4.5139>4.513885$ → **one outlier, value 4.5139 mm**; the minimum 4.4988 stays inside the band. This reproduces the *Escl.* sheet decision.

title: Es01 worked example — recompute after outlier management
prompt:
Outlier management is done by **substitution**: the rejected reading 4.5139 mm is replaced by the mean of the surviving values, the workbook's standard fix. Column **A2:A51** now holds the **cleaned** Es01 set (both the high outlier 4.5139 and the borderline low 4.4988 have been replaced by the recomputed mean, exactly as the *after-outlier-management* workbook does it).

Recompute $n$, the **cleaned mean** $\bar x$ and the **cleaned st. dev.** $s$. These are the numbers every later stage (frequency distribution, $\chi^2$, NPP, $t$-test) uses.
hint: Same AVERAGE / STDEV.S as before, now on the cleaned column. The spread should shrink once the outlier is gone.

given.A1: Es01 cleaned measurements [mm] — after outlier management

checks.label: n
checks.teach: The substitution method keeps $n=50$: the suspect readings are replaced, not deleted, so the count is unchanged.

checks.label: Cleaned mean
checks.teach: The cleaned mean is $\bar x=4.5061823$ mm — barely moved from the raw 4.506192, because the substituted values sit at the mean. This is the value used for the frequency distribution and $\chi^2$.

checks.label: Cleaned st. dev.
checks.teach: The cleaned st. dev. drops to $s=0.0025677$ mm (from 0.0029867): removing the extreme reading **shrinks the spread**, exactly what outlier management should do. Compare with the workbook's *Data* sheet after cleaning.

checks.label: Maximum
checks.teach: The new maximum is 4.5113 mm — the former outlier 4.5139 is gone, and 4.5113 sits comfortably inside the (recomputed) Chauvenet band, so a second pass excludes nothing.

checks.label: Minimum
checks.teach: The new minimum is 4.5020 mm. Both ends now lie inside the limits, so the cleaning is complete.

method: On the cleaned column: $n=50$ (substitution, not deletion); $\bar x=$ AVERAGE $=4.5061823$ mm; $s=$ STDEV.S $=0.0025677$ mm — down from 0.0029867. Max $=4.5113$, min $=4.5020$, both inside the band. These reproduce the *after-outlier-management* Data sheet and feed every later stage.

title: Es01 worked example — frequency-distribution set-up
prompt:
With the cleaned statistics ($n=50$, $\bar x=4.5061823$ mm, $s=0.0025677$ mm, all given), set up the frequency distribution the workbook's *Fr. Distr.* sheet uses to test normality.

The **examined field** spans $\bar x\pm4s$, the **number of classes** is $\sqrt{n}+4$ (rounded to a whole number in practice), and the **class width** is the field length divided by the number of classes.
hint: Field = mean ± 4·s. Number of classes = SQRT(n)+4 ≈ 11. Class width = (fieldMax − fieldMin) / 11.

checks.label: Field min
checks.teach: The field is taken $\pm4s$ around the mean so essentially all of a normal sample falls inside: $x_{min}=\bar x-4s=4.4959115$ mm.

checks.label: Field max
checks.teach: $x_{max}=\bar x+4s=4.5164532$ mm. The field length is $8s$.

checks.label: Number of classes (raw)
checks.teach: The professor's empirical rule $\sqrt{n}+4$ gives $\sqrt{50}+4\approx11.07$. Too few classes hides structure; too many leaves empty bins that break $\chi^2$.

checks.label: Number of classes (rounded)
checks.teach: Rounded to a whole number this is **11 classes**, exactly the workbook's choice for Es01.

checks.label: Class width
checks.teach: Width $=\dfrac{x_{max}-x_{min}}{\#classes}=\dfrac{8s}{11}=0.0018674$ mm. Each class then runs from a lower bound to lower + width, and the observed counts are compared with $n[\Phi(\text{to})-\Phi(\text{from})]$ in the $\chi^2$ test.

method: Field $=[\bar x-4s,\ \bar x+4s]=[4.4959115,\ 4.5164532]$ mm. Classes $=\sqrt{50}+4\approx11.07\to11$. Width $=(4.5164532-4.4959115)/11=0.0018674$ mm. These reproduce the *Fr. Distr.* sheet of the Es01 workbook.
